nativeRange.habitat: Found in deep recesses, caves, and overhangs on coral reefs, generally at depths of roughly 3 to 45 m. The species is considered common throughout its range but is secretive and rarely observed in the open.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a variety of meaty preparations in captivity, including mysis shrimp, finely chopped marine fish flesh, krill, and quality frozen carnivore mixes. Generally advised to feed once or twice daily, only what will be consumed within a few minutes. Tends to ambush small prey from cover rather than chase food in open water.

avoidWith: other Liopropoma basslets (unless mated pair in a larger system)
tiny gobies and other bite-sized fish
large aggressive predators (lionfish, large groupers, moray eels)
boisterous tankmates that out-compete for food

summary: A small, secretive Caribbean reef basslet (Liopropoma rubre) with bold red, yellow, and black banding. Generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe, but tends to spend most of its time tucked into caves and overhangs.
careNotes:
The Swissguard Basslet is a small, cave-dwelling predator from the tropical Western Atlantic. It tends to be highly secretive — field reports describe it as common throughout its range but rarely observed in the open — and in the aquarium it typically spends much of its time hovering near a favored cave entrance or patrolling the lower water column for prey. A mature aquascape with deep caves, overhangs, and shaded crevices is generally advised so the fish feels secure enough to come out and feed regularly.

The species is generally considered reef-safe, though with the usual caveat: it has been known to pick off very small ornamental shrimp and bite-sized fish, so it is best avoided alongside sexy shrimp, very small gobies, or post-larval livestock. Corals and larger invertebrates are typically left alone. It tends to be peaceful toward unrelated tankmates of appropriate size, but is widely considered intolerant of conspecifics and other Liopropoma basslets — best kept singly unless a confirmed mated pair can be sourced for a noticeably larger system.

The Swissguard Basslet has been reported to be a determined jumper, especially when startled; a tightly fitted lid is generally advised.

The species belongs to the family Liopropomatidae — the current accepted classification for this genus — though some retailers and older references may list it under Grammidae or Serranidae.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for general husbandry baseline (water parameters, diet, temperament, reef-safe status). Listed family (Grammidae) was rejected in favor of FishBase's Liopropomatidae.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Liopropomatidae, order Perciformes), max body length (10.0 cm TL), depth range (3–45 m), native distribution, and field behavior (common but secretive).

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked min tank size (30 gal / ~114 L), temperament, diet, water parameters (74–80°F), reef-safe caveats, and cave-dwelling behavior. Designated secondary BlueZooAquatics returned 404; AquariumDomain substituted.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Additional cross-check for min tank size (30 gal), max size (4 in / ~10 cm), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), conspecific aggression note, and jumping/lid warning. Reef2Reef wiki, TFH Magazine, and Fishkeeping World pages for this species all returned 403/404.
